title: Delete given node
description: Delete a node when only that node reference is given

examples.input: List: 1→2→3→4→5, Delete node with value 3
examples.explanation: 1. Given node points to node with value 3. 2. Copy data from next node (value 4) into current node: node.data = 4. 3. Update node.next to point to node.next.next (node with value 5). 4. Node with original value 3 now has value 4 and points to node with value 5. 5. The node with original value 4 is effectively removed.

examples.input: List: 10→20→30, Delete node with value 20
examples.explanation: 1. Given node points to node with value 20. 2. Copy data from next node (value 30) into current node: node.data = 30. 3. Update node.next to point to node.next.next (which is null). 4. Node with original value 20 now has value 30 and points to null. 5. The node with original value 30 is removed.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Copy Next Node Data and Delete Next
approaches.summary: Copy the data from the next node into the given node, then delete the next node by updating the pointer. This works because you cannot directly delete the given node without access to the previous node.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Delete Node When Only Node Reference Is Given
ladders.desc: Complete solution to delete a node from a singly linked list when only that node's reference is provided.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires deleting a node from a singly linked list when we only have access to that specific node, not the head of the list or any previous nodes. The key insight is that we cannot directly delete the given node because we lack access to the previous node's next pointer. Instead, we can simulate deletion by copying data from the next node into the current node, then deleting the next node. This works because we effectively replace the current node's content with the next node's content, then remove the next node, achieving the same result as deleting the current node. The algorithm: 1. Check if the given node or its next node is null (if next is null, this approach won't work for the last node). 2. Copy the data from node.next into node. 3. Update node.next to point to node.next.next, effectively removing the next node. 4. If needed, free the memory of the deleted next node (in languages with manual memory management). Time complexity is O(1) since we perform constant-time operations. Space complexity is O(1) as we use only a few temporary variables. This approach is elegant but has limitations: it doesn't work for the last node in the list, and it doesn't truly delete the given node but rather transforms it. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete deletion logic using the node copying technique.

examples.input: List: 1→2→3→4→5, Delete node with value 3
examples.trace: 1. Given node points to node with value 3. 2. Copy data from next node (value 4) into current node: node.data = 4. 3. Update node.next to point to node.next.next (node with value 5). 4. Node with original value 3 now has value 4 and points to node with value 5. 5. The node with original value 4 is effectively removed.

examples.input: List: 10→20→30, Delete node with value 20
examples.trace: 1. Given node points to node with value 20. 2. Copy data from next node (value 30) into current node: node.data = 30. 3. Update node.next to point to node.next.next (which is null). 4. Node with original value 20 now has value 30 and points to null. 5. The node with original value 30 is removed.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Copy Next Node Data and Update Pointer
ladders.desc: The core operation of copying data from the next node and updating the next pointer.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the deletion approach. It performs two critical operations: copying the data from the next node into the current node, and updating the current node's next pointer to skip the next node. The intuition is that by copying the next node's data, we preserve the list's content sequence, and by updating the pointer, we remove the next node from the chain. This effectively makes the current node take on the identity of the next node, then we discard the original next node. Step-by-step: 1. Verify that the current node and its next node are not null. 2. Copy node.next.data to node.data. 3. Set node.next = node.next.next. 4. Optionally, delete/free the old next node if memory management is needed. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves simple assignments. Space complexity is O(1) using constant extra space. This sub-routine connects to L0 by being the essential function called in the full problem solution. A common mistake is not checking for null pointers, which could lead to errors. Another mistake is trying to use this on the last node, where node.next is null, making copying impossible.

examples.input: Node A with data 5, next pointing to Node B with data 8, which points to Node C with data 12
examples.trace: 1. Node A has data=5, next=B. Node B has data=8, next=C. 2. Copy B.data to A.data: A.data = 8. 3. Update A.next to B.next: A.next = C. 4. Result: A.data=8, A.next=C. Node B is no longer referenced.

examples.input: Node X with data 30, next pointing to Node Y with data 40, which points to null
examples.trace: 1. Node X has data=30, next=Y. Node Y has data=40, next=null. 2. Copy Y.data to X.data: X.data = 40. 3. Update X.next to Y.next: X.next = null. 4. Result: X.data=40, X.next=null. Node Y is removed.

testCases.input: Node data=2, next points to node data=4 next=node data=6
testCases.expected: Node data=4, next points to node data=6

Node data=1, next points to node data=3 next=node data=5
testCases.expected: Node data=3, next points to node data=5

testCases.expected: Node data=9, next points to node data=11

testCases.input: Node data=0, next points to node data=1 next=node data=2
testCases.expected: Node data=1, next points to node data=2

testCases.input: Node data=50, next points to node data=60 next=node data=70
testCases.expected: Node data=60, next points to node data=70

testCases.input: Node data=99, next points to node data=100 next=null
Node data=12, next points to node data=24 next=node data=36
testCases.expected: Node data=24, next points to node data=36

testCases.expected: Node data=16, next points to node data=32

testCases.input: Node data=45, next points to node data=90 next=node data=135
testCases.expected: Node data=90, next points to node data=135

ladders.title: Core Logic — Simulating Deletion via Data Copying
ladders.desc: The central technique of replacing a node's data with its successor's data to achieve deletion.
ladders.explanation: This core logic revolves around the insight that in a singly linked list, deleting a node typically requires modifying the previous node's next pointer. When that's not possible, we can simulate deletion by making the current node impersonate the next node, then removing the next node. The intuition is that the list's order is maintained by the sequence of data values and pointers. By copying the next node's data into the current node, we keep the data sequence intact. Then, by skipping the next node, we remove it from the pointer chain. This works because the data is what matters for the list's logical content, not the specific node objects. Steps: 1. Identify that direct deletion is impossible without previous node access. 2. Copy the data from node.next to node. 3. Bypass node.next by pointing node.next to node.next.next. Time and space are O(1). This logic connects to L1 as it explains why the sub-routine works. A key insight is that this doesn't actually delete the given node but transforms it, which is acceptable as long as the list's data order is preserved. A common mistake is misunderstanding that this only works if there's a next node to copy from.

examples.input: Want to delete node with 'apple', but only have reference to it. Next node has 'banana'.
examples.trace: 1. Current node data='apple', next points to node with 'banana'. 2. Copy 'banana' to current node: data becomes 'banana'. 3. Update next pointer to skip the 'banana' node. 4. Result: Sequence 'apple'→'banana' becomes just 'banana' in the same node position.

examples.input: Delete node with value 100 in list: ...→100→200→...
examples.trace: 1. Node has value 100, next points to node with 200. 2. Copy 200 to current node: value becomes 200. 3. Skip the original 200 node by pointing to its next. 4. List sequence preserved as if 100 was deleted.

testCases.input: Simulate deletion of node data='cat' with next data='dog'
Simulate deletion of node data=25 with next data=50
Simulate deletion of node data='red' with next data='blue'
Simulate deletion of node data=0 with next data=1

Simulate deletion of node data=33 with next data=66
Simulate deletion of node data='start' with next data='end'

ladders.title: Building Block — Accessing and Modifying Node Data and Pointers
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of reading data from a node and updating pointers in a linked list.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers the basic skills needed to manipulate nodes in a singly linked list: accessing a node's data and its next pointer, and modifying them. In a linked list, each node typically has two parts: data (the value stored) and next (a reference to the next node). To read data, you use node.data. To read the next pointer, use node.next. To modify data, assign a new value to node.data. To modify the next pointer, assign a new reference to node.next. The intuition is that these operations allow traversal and restructuring of the list. For example, to copy data from one node to another, you read from nodeA.data and write to nodeB.data. To skip a node, you set node.next to node.next.next. Time complexity for each operation is O(1). Space is O(1). This connects to L2 as it provides the tools to implement the data copying logic. Common mistakes include confusing node.data with node, or not understanding that node.next is a reference, not the data itself. In Python-style pseudocode, these are simple attribute accesses and assignments.

examples.input: Node with data=10, next points to another node. Read its data and next, then update data to 20 and next to null.
examples.trace: 1. Read data: value = node.data → 10. 2. Read next: nxt = node.next → reference to next node. 3. Update data: node.data = 20. 4. Update next: node.next = null. 5. Result: node.data=20, node.next=null.

examples.input: Node A has data=5, next points to Node B with data=8. Copy B's data to A, and set A's next to B's next.
examples.trace: 1. Access B's data: b_data = B.data → 8. 2. Access B's next: b_next = B.next. 3. Modify A's data: A.data = b_data → 8. 4. Modify A's next: A.next = b_next. 5. Result: A now mimics B.

testCases.input: Node data=3, next=nodeX. Read data and next.
Node data='hello', next=null. Update data to 'world'.
Node data=100, next=nodeY. Set next to null.
Node data=1, next=nodeZ. Copy data from nodeZ if nodeZ.data=2.
Node data=7, next=nodeA. Update data to 10 and next to nodeB.
Node data='a', next=node1. Read data and next, then set data='b'.
Node data=0, next=node2. Set next to node2.next if node2.next=node3.
Node data=50, next=nodeP. Update data to 60.
Node data='foo', next=nodeQ. Set next to null and data to 'bar'.
Node data=12, next=nodeR. Copy data from nodeR if nodeR.data=24.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Node Reference and Pointer Assignment
ladders.desc: Understanding how references work and assigning pointers between nodes.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves working with references in a linked list. In many programming languages, a node is an object, and the 'next' pointer is a reference (or pointer) to another node object. A reference is like an address that tells you where the node is in memory. To assign a pointer, you set one reference equal to another. For example, if nodeA.next = nodeB, then nodeA's next pointer now refers to the same node that nodeB refers to. The intuition is that by changing references, you can rearrange the connections between nodes without moving the nodes themselves. This is crucial for operations like skipping a node: if node.next = node.next.next, you're making node point directly to the node after its current next, effectively removing the middle node from the chain. Time and space are O(1). This connects to L3 as it underpins the pointer updates needed for deletion. A common mistake is thinking that assignment copies the node data; it only copies the reference. Another mistake is dereferencing null pointers, which can cause errors.

examples.input: Node X points to Node Y. Node Y points to Node Z. Make X point directly to Z.
examples.trace: 1. Initially: X.next = Y, Y.next = Z. 2. Assign X.next = Y.next (which is Z). 3. Now X.next = Z. 4. Y is still there but not referenced by X.

examples.input: Node A points to Node B. Node B points to null. Make A point to null.
examples.trace: 1. Initially: A.next = B, B.next = null. 2. Assign A.next = B.next (which is null). 3. Now A.next = null. 4. B is still there but not referenced by A.

testCases.input: Node P.next = Q, Q.next = R. Set P.next = R.
Node M.next = N, N.next = null. Set M.next = null.
Node U.next = V, V.next = W. Set U.next = V.next.
Node C.next = D, D.next = E. Set C.next = E.
Node L.next = M, M.next = N. Set L.next = M.next.
Node X.next = Y, Y.next = Z. Set X.next = Z.
Node J.next = K, K.next = null. Set J.next = K.next.
Node F.next = G, G.next = H. Set F.next = H.
Node T.next = U, U.next = V. Set T.next = V.
Node O.next = P, P.next = Q. Set O.next = Q.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What Is a Linked List Node?
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic structure of a node in a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation: A linked list node is a fundamental data structure building block. It's like a container that holds two things: some data (which can be a number, string, object, etc.) and a reference (or pointer) to the next node in the sequence. Think of it as a train car: the data is the cargo inside, and the reference is the coupling that connects to the next car. In a singly linked list, each node only knows about the next node, not the previous one. This structure allows for dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions at certain positions. The intuition is that by linking nodes together via references, you create a chain where you can traverse from one node to the next. For example, if you have Node A with data=5 and next pointing to Node B, you can access B via A.next. Time to access a node's data or next is O(1). Space per node is O(1) for the data and reference. This foundation connects to L4 as it defines what a node is, enabling reference assignments. A common mistake is confusing the node itself with its data or next pointer. Beginners should practice creating nodes and linking them to form simple lists.

examples.input: Create a node with data=10 and no next node.
examples.trace: 1. Define a node structure with attributes data and next. 2. Set data = 10. 3. Set next = null (meaning it doesn't point to anything). 4. Result: node.data=10, node.next=null.

examples.input: Create two nodes: Node1 with data=1, Node2 with data=2. Link Node1 to Node2.
examples.trace: 1. Create Node1: data=1, next=null. 2. Create Node2: data=2, next=null. 3. Set Node1.next = Node2 (this makes Node1 point to Node2). 4. Result: Node1.data=1, Node1.next=Node2; Node2.data=2, Node2.next=null.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Dummy Head with Traversal
approaches.summary: Create a dummy head node and traverse from the head to find the previous node of the given node, then delete it by updating pointers. This simulates having access to the full list.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Delete Node with Dummy Head Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution to delete a node when only that node reference is given, using dummy head traversal approach.
ladders.explanation: The core challenge is deleting a node in a singly linked list when we only have access to that specific node (not the head). Normally, deletion requires modifying the previous node's next pointer, but we can't directly access the previous node. The dummy head approach solves this by creating a temporary dummy node that points to the actual head, then traversing from this dummy head to find the node whose next pointer points to our target node. Once we find this previous node, we can perform standard deletion by updating its next pointer to skip the target node. The intuition is that by starting from a dummy head, we ensure we have a consistent starting point even if the target node is the actual head, and we can traverse to find the previous node relationship. Step-by-step: 1. Create a dummy node with next pointing to head. 2. Initialize current pointer at dummy. 3. Traverse while current.next exists and current.next is not the target node. 4. If current.next is target, update current.next = current.next.next to delete. 5. Return dummy.next as new head. Time: O(n) worst-case traversal. Space: O(1) for dummy node and pointers. This feeds into the full problem by providing a complete working solution. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like target being head or last node, or infinite loops if target not found. Real code pattern: def deleteNode(dummy_head, target): current = dummy_head; while current.next and current.next != target: current = current.next; if current.next: current.next = current.next.next; return dummy_head.next.

examples.input: List: 1->2->3->4, Target: node with value 3
examples.trace: 1. Create dummy node -> head (1). 2. current = dummy. 3. current.next is 1, not target, move current to 1. 4. current.next is 2, not target, move current to 2. 5. current.next is 3 (target), update current.next = current.next.next (4). 6. Return dummy.next (1). Result: 1->2->4.

examples.input: List: 5->10, Target: node with value 5
examples.trace: 1. Create dummy node -> head (5). 2. current = dummy. 3. current.next is 5 (target), update current.next = current.next.next (10). 4. Return dummy.next (10). Result: 10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Previous Node Using Dummy Head
ladders.desc: Traverse from dummy head to locate the node immediately before a target node.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the deletion approach: given a dummy head node (which points to the actual list head) and a target node, we need to find the node whose next pointer points to the target. The intuition is that by starting at dummy, we can systematically check each node's next pointer until we either find the target or reach the end. This works because in a singly linked list, the only way to identify the previous node is by traversing from the beginning and checking relationships. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize current pointer at dummy. 2. While current.next exists and current.next is not the target: move current to current.next. 3. After loop, current points to node before target if found, or last node if not. Time: O(n) for traversal. Space: O(1) for pointer. This feeds into L0 by providing the mechanism to locate the deletion point. Common mistakes include infinite loops if not checking current.next existence, or missing the case where target is head (dummy handles this). Real code pattern: def findPrev(dummy, target): current = dummy; while current.next and current.next != target: current = current.next; return current.

examples.input: Dummy->1->2->3, Target: node 2
examples.trace: 1. current = dummy. 2. current.next is 1, not target, move current to 1. 3. current.next is 2 (target), stop. current is node 1.

examples.input: Dummy->5->10, Target: node 5
examples.trace: 1. current = dummy. 2. current.next is 5 (target), stop. current is dummy.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pointer Manipulation to Skip a Node
ladders.desc: Update a node's next pointer to bypass another node, effectively deleting it from the chain.
ladders.explanation: The central technique in linked list deletion is pointer manipulation: to remove a node, we modify the previous node's next pointer to point to the node after the target, skipping it. The intuition is that in a singly linked list, nodes are connected only via next pointers; by redirecting these pointers, we can exclude a node from the traversal path without actually erasing it from memory. This works because subsequent traversals will follow the updated pointers and never reach the skipped node. Step-by-step: Given a previous node prev and target node to delete: 1. Check if prev.next exists and equals target. 2. If yes, set prev.next = target.next. 3. This detaches target from the list. Time: O(1) for pointer update. Space: O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the deletion action once previous node is found. Common mistakes include not checking prev.next existence, or losing reference to target.next before updating. Real code pattern: if prev.next == target: prev.next = target.next.

examples.input: Prev node points to target (value 3), target.next is 4
examples.trace: 1. prev.next is node 3. 2. target.next is node 4. 3. Update prev.next = target.next (4). Result: prev->4, node 3 is skipped.

examples.input: Prev is dummy, target is head (value 1), target.next is 2
examples.trace: 1. dummy.next is node 1. 2. target.next is node 2. 3. Update dummy.next = target.next (2). Result: dummy->2, node 1 is skipped.

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing a Linked List with a While Loop
ladders.desc: Iterate through nodes in a linked list using a pointer and a loop condition.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves moving through a linked list node by node to access or find specific elements. The intuition is that since nodes are sequentially connected via next pointers, we can start at the head and repeatedly move to the next node until we reach the end (null). This works because each node holds a reference to the next, allowing linear traversal. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize current pointer at head. 2. While current is not null: process current node, then set current = current.next. 3. Stop when current becomes null. Time: O(n) for n nodes. Space: O(1) for pointer. This feeds into L2 by enabling the search for the previous node. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating current, or off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: current = head; while current: # do something; current = current.next.

examples.trace: 1. current = 1. 2. Process 1, move current to 2. 3. Process 2, move current to 3. 4. Process 3, move current to null. Stop.

examples.input: List: 5
examples.trace: 1. current = 5. 2. Process 5, move current to null. Stop.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Node Pointers (next)
ladders.desc: Retrieve or modify the next pointer of a linked list node.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves working with the next attribute of a node, which stores the reference to the subsequent node in the list. The intuition is that in a singly linked list, each node contains data and a next pointer; by accessing node.next, we can navigate or change connections. This works because pointers are essentially memory addresses linking nodes. Step-by-step: For a given node: 1. To read: next_node = node.next. 2. To update: node.next = new_node. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This feeds into L3 by enabling traversal and pointer updates. Common mistakes include accessing next on a null node, or confusing assignment. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.next = None. Then use node.next.

examples.input: Node A with next pointing to B
examples.trace: 1. Node A has attribute next. 2. A.next returns reference to node B.

examples.input: Update node X's next to point to Y
examples.trace: 1. Initially X.next may be null or another node. 2. Execute X.next = Y. 3. Now X.next references Y.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Singly Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understand the basic building block of a linked list: a node with data and a next pointer.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a singly linked list node is a data structure that holds a value (data) and a reference (pointer) to the next node in the sequence. The intuition is that it's like a chain where each link knows about the next link, allowing dynamic storage without contiguous memory. This works because each node is an object with attributes; the next pointer can be null to indicate the end. Step-by-step: Define a node with two fields: val (e.g., integer) and next (reference to another node or null). Time: O(1) to create. Space: O(1) per node. This feeds into L4 by providing the object whose pointers we access. Common mistakes include not initializing next to null, or misunderstanding references vs. values. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.next = None.

examples.input: Create a node with value 42
examples.trace: 1. Instantiate Node class with val=42. 2. next is set to None by default. Result: node object with val 42 and no next node.

examples.input: Create two nodes: A=5, B=10, link A to B
examples.trace: 1. Create node A with val=5, next=None. 2. Create node B with val=10, next=None. 3. Set A.next = B. Now A points to B.
